Usually, `copyto!` uses `memmove`, so I would always expect it to work correctly with aliasing. However, if the type is not a bitstype(union), the detection of overlapping regions works incorrectly. Example:
```julia
test = Vector{Tuple{Int,BigInt}}(undef, 5);
test[1] = (1, BigInt(1));
test[2] = (2, BigInt(2));
test[3] = (3, BigInt(3));
copyto!(test, 3, test, 1, 3);
test
```
This should yield `[(1, 1), (2, 2), (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)]`, but the last element is actually `(1, 1)` again. I think the only issue is line 296:
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/67dffc4a8aed0ba4441387af4dfdbad05908473c/base/array.jl#L293-L296
where the check for an overlap should actually be `n * sizeof(eltype(src))`, which fixes the issue (assuming that `_unsafe_copyto!` is always called with `Array`s or at least something that has an `eltype` - is this safe?).

{"action":"opened","number":54324,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54324","id":1850075986,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5uRetS","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54324","number":54324,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix docstring search by signatures revisited","user":{"login":"projekter","id":2815453,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4MTU0NTM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2815453?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter","html_url":"https://github.com/projekter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing The only way to circumvent this possible incompatibility is if we decide to formally allow this syntax for functions. (But then, it should be documented somewhere.)
Then, it should also formally be defined show this syntax is interpreted. If we just say that in the documentation, `fun{X}(args)` is _exactly_ equivalent to `fun(args) where {X}`, then it is clear that everything in `X` is a type variable (with possible subtyping). Apart from the case in which `fun` is the name of a type/constructor, as then it could also be a concrete type (or some other bitstype). I previously thought that it is not easily possible for the macro-called function determining which is the case, but as I now think of it, I'm no longer so sure. For outer constructors, for sure when the macro is called, the type must already exist. And in order for the Julia compiler to accept `fun{X}` without `X` being in the `where` clause, `X` must also be well defined (in the context of the calling module, which is available to the macro). I don't know how it is when the docstring is attached to an inner constructor, though.
Then, in the specification of what `fun{X}(args)` means when `fun` is a constructor, should we allow mixing actual generic types (coming first?) with parametric types from the arguments (pretty clumsy IMHO)? Or just disallow this shorthand is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing Define
```julia
struct UVec{T} <: AbstractVector{T}
    data::Vector{T}
end

Base.IndexStyle(::Type{<:UVec}) = IndexLinear()
Base.size(x::UVec) = (UInt(length(x.data)),)
Base.@propagate_inbounds Base.getindex(x::UVec, i...) = getindex(x.data, i...)
```
and then display `UVec(collect(Iterators.repeated(missing, 100)))` on the console (depending on the height of your console, the number of elements can be chosen smaller, it just matters that the vdots ellipsis is shown).

Some observations while constructing this MWE:
- In my case, that my custom vector had unsigned indices was not intended. The `size` of the vector was given by the length of a UnitRange with unsigned elements, which happens to be unsigned. That makes sense, though I had the impression that `length` always gives an `Int`, so this was unexpected.
- In my case, my custom vector delivered custom elements. For the MWE, I used `missing`. The error does not occur if the elements are numbers; basically, it does not occur whenever `Base.alignment(io, <type of the element>)` is defined not to fall back to the generic version. This is because then, `A[k][1]`, i.e., the number of characters before the alignment, is greater than zero and `length(vdots) = 1`, so no underflow can occur.
- I also find the following lines interesting:
  https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/a3f710e2a35402bb7f7f96f874e6c696dfc911da/base/arrayshow.jl#L64-L77
  In each iteration, `aij` is asserted to be a `Tuple{Int,Int}`; but then, the eltype of `a` is `Tuple{T,V}`. And in the last line, the sum is over elements of `a`, so those are `promote_type(T, V, sep, Int)`. If the alignment is always a 2-tuple of `Int`s, then why store it as a `Tuple{T,V}` at all? Why should the alignment of the rows/cols depend on the index type of the rows/cols at all?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2029549524/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50707","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50707/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50707/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50707/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50707","id":1826589576,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5s34uI","number":50707,"title":"Closure with multiple methods cannot have multiple variable capture blocks","user":{"login":"projekter","id":2815453,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4MTU0NTM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2815453?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter","html_url":"https://github.com/projekter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-07-28T15:04:57Z","updated_at":"2023-07-28T15:04:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The following should be valid Julia syntax:
```julia
function failure(cval)
    function test end
    test(x) = nothing
    let cval=cval
        test(x, i) = cval
    end
end
failure(1)
```
However, the parser fails in line 3 (which is the _first_ definition of `test`) with an
> UndefVarError: `cval` not defined

Various observations:
- Obviously, line 3 does not even contain `cval`, so if there was any error, it would be in line 4.
- Capturing the value of `cval` works fine if the first definition of `test` is removed.
- Capturing the value of `cval` also works fine if the second definition is moved before the first one (this is why I explictly defined the function, so that it is already in the scope of `failure`). This is not a viable workaround if both definitions need a capture. The general scheme seems to be: Such a `let`-block for capturing variables is only possible if the given function has no previous methods.
- If I move both definitions into the same `let` block, it also works. I would suggest that such an example is added to the [documentation](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/performance-tips/#man-performance-captured), as I first thought that the use of the same variable among multiple independent methods will lead to the same boxing behavior just to be on the safe side. But apparently, Julia can still optimize this very nicely - I guess that because `cval` does not leak outside of the function, all its potential types and values are known even at an early stage in the compilation process.
- I can guess that the issue comes from the fact that the types of the captured variables are encoded in the type of the function that is returned. And the `let` block seems to be responsible for finally fixing the function type - so after the first capture, it is not possible to capture anything else, as it would need to change the type again (well, except if we'd capture the same variables or a subset, which still leads to an error). This means that my code example actually _is_ invalid Julia syntax due to an implementation detail that is not mentioned in the documentation. However, then the error message is completely wrong and should rather hint to this syntax currently not being possible.

I'm using the official binary image.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47977","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47977/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47977/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47977/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47977","id":1509163806,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Z9AMe","number":47977,"title":"Documentation: sortperm! with initialized=false","user":{"login":"projekter","id":2815453,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4MTU0NTM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2815453?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter","html_url":"https://github.com/projekter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-12-23T10:21:50Z","updated_at":"2022-12-23T10:21:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The documentation of `sortperm!` says:

> Like [sortperm](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/sort/#Base.sortperm), but accepts a preallocated index vector `ix`. If initialized is `false` (the default), `ix` is initialized to contain the values `1:length(v)`.

Maybe the innocent word "index" is already supposed to tell the reader exactly what to expect from this function. But it did not for me. I would even say that an index vector `ix` is any vector that contains nonnegative integers, since for any such `ix` we can find another vector `x` such that `x[ix]` is a valid indexing operation.

So here's my expectation: `v` can be put to a certain order, and usually this is what `v[ix]` should do. However, the fact that we can switch off initialization at all suggests that `ix = 1:length(v)` is a prerequisite for `sortperm!` to behave like this. And if `ix` contains other values, then these other values are put in the same order as `1:length(v)` would have been put. Example:
```julia
julia> sortperm!([5,6,7,8], [4,3,2,1], initialized=true)
4-element Vector{Int64}:
 8
 7
 6
 5
```
The vector `[4,3,2,1]` can be sorted by reversing the order. So usually, `sortperm!` would reverse `1:4`, but now it has to reverse `5:8`.
If you check what the example does, you may indeed find that my expectations are fulfilled. However, just running it multiple times will show that you can really get _anything_:
```julia
julia> import StatsBase
julia> sort!(collect(StatsBase.countmap([sortperm!([5,6,7,8], [4,3,2,1], initialized=true) for _ in 1:100000])))
24-element Vector{Pair{Vector{Int64}, Int64}}:
 [5, 6, 7, 8] => 93038
 [5, 6, 8, 7] => 103
 [5, 7, 6, 8] => 940
 [5, 7, 8, 6] => 77
 [5, 8, 6, 7] => 61
 [5, 8, 7, 6] => 2447
 [6, 5, 7, 8] => 7
 [6, 5, 8, 7] => 17
 [6, 7, 5, 8] => 1
 [6, 7, 8, 5] => 30
 [6, 8, 5, 7] => 17
 [6, 8, 7, 5] => 2
 [7, 5, 6, 8] => 432
 [7, 5, 8, 6] => 14
 [7, 6, 5, 8] => 332
 [7, 6, 8, 5] => 13
 [7, 8, 5, 6] => 14
 [7, 8, 6, 5] => 5
 [8, 5, 6, 7] => 22
 [8, 5, 7, 6] => 1314
 [8, 6, 5, 7] => 9
 [8, 6, 7, 5] => 12
 [8, 7, 5, 6] => 184
 [8, 7, 6, 5] => 909
```
Indeed, there are 24 permutations of four distinct elements, and you can get all of them. Some are more likely than others... Well, quicksort is unstable, but for unique input vectors, I'd still expect unique outputs.

So, first lesson that should either go into the documentation or into a rewrite of the function: `ix` must be a subset of `1:length(v)`, else the results are nondeterministic.

Second question: What happens if `ix` is such a subset, but it is unordered?
```julia
julia> while true
    tmp = sortperm!([2,3,1,4], [4,3,2,1], initialized=true)
    tmp == [4,3,2,1] || break
end
```
This looks like it never terminates. So actually, the order in which the indices are initially stored is completely irrelevant - this should also go into the documentation, as it tells the user when initialization is actually not necessary.

So then the final usage instructions for `initialized` are: Use `false` whenever your `ix` is not a subset of `1:length(v)`, else the results will be nondeterministic. Use `true` whenever they are such a subset, in whatever order. In particular, `sortperm!` with `initialized=true` can be called multiple times in a row with different vectors (of the same size), but `ix` does not need to be re-initialized.

    item::AbstractVector{<:Union{P,<:AbstractMatrix{P}}}

    function Problem{P}(item::AbstractVector{<:Union{P,<:AbstractMatrix{P}}}) where {P}
        return new{P}(item)
    end
end

struct Container
    el::Problem
end

struct Failure
    function Failure(cnt::Container)
        element = cnt.el
        return typeof(element)(element.item)
    end
end

prob = Problem{Int}(Int[])
cnt = Container(prob)
Failure(cnt)
```

The construction of `Failure` in the last line triggers the error. Notably, only on Windows (Julia 1.7.3 x64), when running on Linux (Ubuntu WSL 1.7.3 x64), the code passes through. Of course, using the Debugger where everything is interpreted also works fine.
Some observations:
- If I delete the constructor for `Problem`, the problem vanishes. Unfortunately, the actual constructor does a bit of work, so that this is not an option.
- If I change `Failure` to accept a `Problem` directly, without the `Container`, the problem vanishes.
- If the `item` is of a more simple type - e.g., replacing the Union by either `P` or `<:AbstractMatrix{P}` - the problem vanishes.
- I was very surprised when I ended up with this MWE, since a construction like this (with a type like `item`, a container, a `typeof`-constructor call, the internal constructor like this) has been part of my code for weeks now and always worked. I did some other changes completely unrelated to this, which somehow ended up raising this error. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45105","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45105/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45105/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45105/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45105","id":1217329164,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5IjvgM","number":45105,"title":"`methods` with type restriction documentation","user":{"login":"projekter","id":2815453,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4MTU0NTM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/2815453?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter","html_url":"https://github.com/projekter","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/projekter/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-04-27T13:03:04Z","updated_at":"2022-04-27T13:03:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The documentation for `methods` says that "if [the second argument] `types` is specified, return an array of methods whose types match." I did not realize that this explanation is actually not as clear as it sounds until I encountered some behavior that I found strange and did some experiments. I would suggest that the meaning of "match" is expanded, as there are multiple ways to explain this:
- First attempt. Return only the (single) method that has exactly the same type signature - no subtype checks are performed, possibly return empty array. The most narrow definition of "match".
    ```Julia
    > function match1(::Integer) end;
    > methods(match1, (Int,))
    # 1 method for generic function match1:
    * match1(::Integer) in Main at In[1]:1
    ```
    Apparently, this is not meant, as `Int != Integer`, but `Int <: Integer`.
- Second attempt. Return all methods which, when looked at in isolation, would be callable using the argument types (though there might be more specific ones which could be called instead). This interpretation is supported by the documentation for `Base.invoke`, which contains the sentence "This method allows invoking a method other than the most specific matching method" and hence implies that there are other "matching methods" than the "most specific" one.
    ```Julia
    > function match2(::Integer) end;
    > function match2(::Int) end;
    > methods(match2, (Int,))
    # 1 method for generic function match2:
    * match2(::Int64) in Main at In[2]:2
    ```
    So this also does not seem to be the case, the first method is not listed although it matches according to this definition.
- Third attempt. Return only the most specific method that would be called with these types, or an empty vector if there is no one. This is already supported by the first two code examples. What could make this different from `which` is that in the case of ambiguities, we could get all possible "matching" methods.
    ```Julia
    > abstract type type3A end;
    > abstract type type3B <: type3A end;
    > abstract type type3C <: type3A end;
    > function match3(::type3B) end;
    > function match3(::type3C) end;
    > methods(match3, (type3A,))
    # 2 methods for generic function match3:
    * match3(::type3B) in Main at In[3]:4
    * match3(::type3C) in Main at In[3]:5
    ```
    And indeed, in this case `which` throws an error: "no unique matching method found for the specified argument types". So this seems to be the correct interpretation - which can lead to the (surprising) fact that adding a more specific method can reduce the number of methods listed, and that deleting a more specific method can in fact increase the number of methods (the last case what brought this to my attention).
